claim: Every Midnight transaction contains a guaranteed_zswap_offer that is always present and collects fees for all transaction phases
Every Midnight transaction contains an optional fallible_zswap_offer that may fail without affecting the guaranteed section
Every Midnight transaction contains an optional contract_calls_or_deploys field for contract interactions or deployments
Every Midnight transaction contains a binding_randomness field that opens the homomorphic Pedersen commitment
The guaranteed section must succeed or the entire transaction is rejected
The fallible section may fail without affecting the guaranteed section
The fallible Zswap offer's coin operations (commitments and nullifiers) are applied during the guaranteed phase
Only fallible contract call effects are rolled back on failure
Each ContractCall contains both guaranteed and fallible transcripts split via the ckpt opcode within a single call
ContractCall contains a contract_address field of type ContractAddress (Bytes<32>)
ContractCall contains an entry_point field of type String
Transaction binding uses homomorphic Pedersen commitments rather than a simple hash
The ledger structure contains zswap_state with a commitment_tree (MerkleTree), commitment_tree_first_free (u32), nullifiers (Set<CoinNullifier>), and commitment_tree_history (Set<MerkleTreeRoot>)
ContractState has a state field of type ImpactValue and an operations field of type Map<String, SNARKVerifierKey>
Contract Merkle trees are MerkleTree(d) Impact values with compile-time-fixed depth, stored as part of the Impact state
Transaction processing has three phases: Well-formedness Check (stateless), Guaranteed Execution (stateful), and Fallible Execution (stateful, may fail)
ZK proof verification occurs during the well-formedness check (stateless phase)
Contract call ZK proofs are verified in the guaranteed phase, not during the well-formedness check
For guaranteed offer balance: for each token type, sum(inputs) - sum(outputs) - fees + mints >= 0
For merging transactions, at least one transaction must have empty contract calls
Contract Address = Hash(contract_state, nonce)
Token Type = Hash(contract_address, domain_separator)
Coin Commitment = Hash<(CoinInfo, CoinPublicKey)>
Nullifier = Hash<(CoinInfo, CoinSecretKey)>
Pedersen commitment formula: Commit(v) = v*G + r*H where G and H are independent generator points on the elliptic curve
There is one Schnorr proof per transaction
The Schnorr proof proves that the contract contribution to the transaction has zero net value
SchnorrProof structure contains: commitment (Point), challenge (Scalar), response (Scalar)
The Schnorr challenge uses the Fiat-Shamir transform
Coin commitment insertion places the commitment at position commitment_tree_first_free and then increments commitment_tree_first_free
The commitment_tree_history window covers a configurable number of recent blocks and old roots expire over time
The nullifiers set must persist forever to prevent double-spend and cannot be pruned
The Compact type Boolean maps to a Field (0 or 1) in state representation
Input structure contains: nullifier (Bytes<32>), type_value_commit (Bytes<32>), contract_address (Option<ContractAddress>), merkle_root (Bytes<32>), merkle_path (Vec<Bytes<32>>), zk_proof (Proof)
Output structure contains: commitment (Bytes<32>), type_value_commit (Bytes<32>), contract_address (Option<ContractAddress>), ciphertext (Option<Bytes>), zk_proof (Proof)
Transcript structure contains: gas_bound (u64), and effects including claimed_nullifiers, received_commitments, spent_commitments, contract_calls_claimed, mints, and program (ImpactProgram)
The Schnorr proof prevents value injection attacks where contracts could create value from nothing
CoinCommitment = Hash<(CoinInfo, ZswapCoinPublicKey)>
CoinNullifier = Hash<(CoinInfo, ZswapCoinSecretKey)>
CoinInfo = { value, type, nonce }
The nullifier is computed from the raw coin data and the spending key, not from the commitment
The Zswap state historic roots are typed TimeFilterMap<MerkleTreeRoot> with time-based expiry
Token types are 256-bit collision-resistant hashes
The native token type identifier is the 256-bit zero value
The native token type identifier is retrieved via nativeToken(): Bytes<32>
Custom token types are computed as Hash(contract_address, domain_separator)
The commitment tree is append-only: commitment_tree_first_free never decreases
The commitment tree history is typed TimeFilterMap<MerkleTreeRoot> and entries expire based on time window
Contract address is computed as Hash(contract_state, nonce), derived from the initial contract state and a nonce
MerkleTree state type stores only the root on-chain and its contents are private
Pedersen commitments are used separately for balance proofs, not as Merkle tree leaves
The entire UTXO is consumed on spending -- partial spends are impossible. Change is returned as a new UTXO
Midnight maintains a TimeFilterMap<MerkleTreeRoot> of valid past roots with time-based expiry
Midnight keeps the nullifier set append-only (does not prune spent outputs, unlike Bitcoin)
Bitcoin's Merkle tree contains transactions, while Midnight's Merkle tree contains coin commitments
persistentCommit<T> signature is (value: T, rand: Bytes<32>): Bytes<32>
persistentHash<T> signature is (value: T): Bytes<32>
transientCommit<T> signature is (value: T, rand: Field): Field
transientHash<T> signature is (value: T): Field
persistentCommit and persistentHash use SHA-256 and produce stable outputs across compiler upgrades
transientCommit and transientHash are circuit-optimized but their algorithm may change between compiler versions
persistentCommit clears witness taint on both input and result; no disclose() needed for the ledger write
Hash functions do not clear witness taint because hash outputs could theoretically be brute-forced
Compact provides hash-based commitments, not algebraic Pedersen commitments
Pedersen commitments (v*G + r*H) are used internally by Zswap for balance proofs, not by Compact's persistentCommit
Set.member() argument must be public, so disclose() is needed when the argument is witness-derived
A two-step cast is required for Uint<64> to Bytes<32>: (round as Field) as Bytes<32>; direct Uint<64> to Bytes<32> cast is invalid
HistoricMerkleTree.checkRoot() accepts proofs against any prior version of the tree
With plain MerkleTree, each insertion changes the root and invalidates all existing proofs
MerkleTree.insert() hides the leaf value argument on-chain
MerkleTreePath<N, T> struct has fields leaf: T and path: Vector<N, MerkleTreePathEntry>
MerkleTreePathEntry has fields sibling: MerkleTreeDigest and goes_left: Boolean
MerkleTreePath has no .value field
There is no historicMember method on HistoricMerkleTree; use checkRoot only
HistoricMerkleTree<N, T> holds at most 2^N leaves; depth 16 supports 65,536 members; depth 20 supports about 1 million
Counter.read() returns Uint<64>
Comparison operators (>=, <=, >, <) only work on Uint<N>, not Field
Which exported circuit was called is always visible to an on-chain observer (circuit name is part of the transaction)
Map and Set operation arguments (keys, values, and elements) are public, with the exception that MerkleTree.insert() hides its leaf argument
generateRandomness() and generateSecureRandom() do not exist in Compact; randomness must come from a witness
findPathForLeaf(leaf) performs an O(n) scan through all leaves
pathForLeaf(index, leaf) performs O(log n) path construction when the leaf's index is known
The number of MerkleTree insertions is observable (the tree index increments visibly)
Midnight defines three core JSON schemas: the Compact AST, transactions, and zero-knowledge proofs
The Proof definition type property is an enum with values groth16 and plonk
The ZK proof schema proofSystem property is an enum with values groth16, plonk, and halo2
The ZK proof schema curve property is an enum with values bn254 and bls12-381
The ZK proof proof object element b is an array of exactly 2 arrays of 2 strings representing a G2 point
The transaction schema hash property is of type string with pattern ^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{64}$
The transaction schema has required properties: version, hash, contractAddress, circuitName, inputs, proof, publicOutputs, stateChanges, timestamp, sender, nonce, fee
The Compact compiler produces ASTs conforming to the Compact AST schema
Midnight uses two foundational protocols: Kachina for data-protecting smart contracts and Zswap for shielded token transfers
Zswap is based on Zerocash
Token type in Zswap is computed as Hash(domain_separator, contract_address)
Kachina uses only non-interactive zero-knowledge proofs
Kachina operates within the Universal Composability (UC) framework
In Kachina, proof sizes are sublinear with respect to circuit complexity
In Kachina, verification time is constant per proof (milliseconds)
Zswap extends Zerocash with native token support (multi-asset) and atomic swaps
Coin commitments in Zswap use a hash function, not Pedersen commitments
type_value_commit uses Pedersen commitments and exists solely for balance verification: type_value_commit = type*G_t + value*G_v + randomness*G_r
The receiveShielded function takes a ShieldedCoinInfo argument and returns []
The sendShielded function takes QualifiedShieldedCoinInfo, recipient: Either<ZswapCoinPublicKey, ContractAddress>, value: Uint<128>; returns SendResult
The mintUnshieldedToken function takes domainSep: Bytes<32>, value: Uint<64>, recipient: Either<ContractAddress, UserAddress>; returns Bytes<32>
Zswap offers merge non-interactively
Coins in Zswap can specify a contract address, and only that contract can spend them
For balance verification in Zswap, for each token type: sum(input_values) = sum(output_values) + fees
Homomorphic balance verification uses Pedersen value commitments: sum(input_type_value_commits) - sum(output_type_value_commits) = Commit(delta)
The contract section's zero-value contribution is proven via a Schnorr proof (one per transaction)
Contracts are written in Compact and compiled to Impact VM bytecode for on-chain execution and ZK circuits for off-chain proof generation
Each ledger field is declared individually with export ledger
Return type [] (empty tuple) indicates no return value
Witnesses are declaration-only in Compact -- they have no body
The implementation of witness functions is in TypeScript, running off-chain
There is no function keyword in Compact
The optional constructor() block runs once at deployment to initialize ledger state
The constructor has no parameters, no return type declaration, and no export or circuit keyword
Field is a finite field element (~254 bits) and occupies 1 field element
Uint<N> occupies ceil(N/254) field elements
Bytes<N> occupies ceil(N/31) field elements
Void does not exist in Compact -- use []
Address is not a primitive in Compact -- use Bytes<32> or ContractAddress/ZswapCoinPublicKey from stdlib
Use Vector<N, T> for fixed-size arrays. The syntax Field[5] is invalid
Only const exists for local variables in Compact. There is no let keyword
Token operations are imported via import CompactStandardLibrary; -- there is no selective import syntax
Every .compact file needs: pragma language_version 0.22;
for loop syntax is for (const i of 0..10) { } -- const, of keyword, parentheses required
Map uses .lookup(key) to read and .insert(key, value) to write (no bracket access)
Set uses .member(value) to check membership (not .contains())
The ledger { } block syntax was removed in Compact 0.10.1 and causes a parse error
export sealed ledger is used for fields that should only be written during contract initialization
MerkleTree supports depths 2 through 32 (2 <= d <= 32)
Map .lookup(key) returns the value, or zero/empty if key not present
Division is available on Field only (modular inverse)
Direct Uint<N> to Bytes<N> cast is invalid -- cast through Field first
persistentHash takes exactly ONE typed argument; to hash multiple values, wrap them in a Vector
Enum values use dot notation: Phase.commit (not Phase::commit)
Phase 1 (Well-Formedness) is stateless: it does not read from or write to the ledger
Effects from phase 2 (Guaranteed) always persist, even if phase 3 fails
Phase 3 (Fallible) failure does NOT revert guaranteed phase effects
The ckpt opcode marks the boundary between guaranteed and fallible sections within a contract call
Contract calls within a transaction execute sequentially; each call sees state changes from previous calls
Every Impact VM operation has a fixed gas cost; if execution exceeds the declared gas bound, the transcript fails
The Impact VM is stack-based and operates on a stack of values
The Impact VM is non-Turing-complete: no backward jumps, guaranteeing termination
The VM stack is initialized with three elements: [Context, Effects, State]
The Impact VM supports value types: Cell, Array(n) where 0 < n < 16, Map, and MerkleTree(d) where 1 <= d <= 32
The context is an Array with 5 entries: contract address, new coin allocations, block timestamp, timestamp divergence bound, block hash
Currently only first two context entries (contract address and new coin allocations) are correctly initialized; entries 2-4 not yet correctly populated
The program counter starts at 0 and can only increase; all iteration is unrolled at compile time
Compact code compiles to two outputs: Impact bytecode (public state changes) and ZK circuits (private computation)
Midnight uses a dual-token model: NIGHT for value/governance, DUST for transaction fees
NIGHT has a fixed supply of 24 billion tokens (non-inflationary)
1 NIGHT = 1,000,000 STARs
NIGHT is unshielded (public)
NIGHT is native on both Cardano and Midnight
DUST is non-transferable between users
DUST is shielded (private)
DUST is continuously generated from NIGHT holdings
DUST decays when disassociated from NIGHT
Block reward formula: Actual Reward = Base Reward x [S + (1 - S) x U] where S=95% at launch, target U=50%
Tokens are distributed for free — there is no token sale
The Glacier Drop lasts 60 days
Glacier Drop allocates 50% to Cardano holders, 20% to Bitcoin holders, 30% to other chain holders
Glacier Drop eligibility requires minimum portfolio value of $100 USD at snapshot time
The Scavenger Mine lasts 30 days
The Lost-and-Found phase runs for 4 years after the Scavenger Mine
Midnight uses ZK SNARKs (Zero-Knowledge Succinct Non-interactive Arguments of Knowledge)
A circuit is an arithmetic circuit — a directed acyclic graph of addition and multiplication gates over a finite field
Proof size is small (less than a kilobyte)
The Compact compilation pipeline is: Compact Source → Compiler → Circuit IR → ZKIR → Proving/Verification Keys
ZKIR is the final circuit representation optimized for the PLONK proving system
In Compact, ledger reads become public inputs in the circuit
In Compact, values returned by witness functions become the witness (private inputs) in the circuit
Verification is constant time regardless of circuit size (the succinct property)
Midnight uses the PLONK proving system with polynomial commitments
PLONK uses gate-based arithmetization rather than constraint matrices
PLONK enforces copy constraints via a permutation argument using a grand product check
PLONK uses a universal Structured Reference String (SRS) — a single trusted setup that works for ALL circuits up to a maximum size
Per-circuit proving and verification keys are derived from the universal SRS during compilation — no per-circuit ceremony needed
Proof generation cost is proportional to circuit size
Verification involves checking polynomial commitment evaluations via pairing checks

The concept-explainer agent loads exactly six skills: core-concepts:data-models, core-concepts:zero-knowledge, core-concepts:privacy-patterns, core-concepts:smart-contracts, core-concepts:protocols, core-concepts:architecture
